Age-related disorders are diseases whose incidence increases with age.
Aging-related inflammation refers to the chronic, low-grade inflammatory state associated with aging.
Disease-causing insertions refer to genetic disorders resulting from the insertion of transposable elements into functional genomic regions.
Genomic disorders are a class of genetic diseases caused by structural variations in the genome, often mediated by segmental duplications or repetitive elements.
Genetic diseases that are passed down from parents to offspring.
Human pathogenesis refers to the process by which a disease develops in humans.
A category of human diseases caused by the insertion of retrotransposable elements into the genome.
Insertional mutagenesis is a process where the insertion of genetic material disrupts gene function, potentially leading to disease.
Mammalian aging refers to the complex biological process of functional decline over time in mammals.
The Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) class I region is strongly associated with more than 100 different diseases.
Normal aging refers to the natural, time-dependent accumulation of physiological and neurological changes that occur in the absence of specific disease.
Repeat expansion diseases are a class of disorders characterized by the instability of causative repeat mutations.
A class of diseases associated with cellular senescence.
Monogenic diseases are disorders caused by mutations in a single gene.
Genetic diseases caused by new mutations that are not inherited from parents.
Sporadic genetic diseases are genetic disorders that occur in individuals with no family history of the condition, often due to de novo mutations.
Various severe diseases can be caused by LINE-1 retrotransposition.
Early spontaneous miscarriage refers to pregnancy loss occurring in the early stages of gestation.
First trimester miscarriage refers to the spontaneous loss of a pregnancy before 13 weeks of gestation.
Chemoresistance is the resistance of cancer cells to chemotherapy drugs.
Drug tolerance is a state of reduced responsiveness to a drug following repeated exposure, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect.
